## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Next: apcs-combining-algorithm (₢A9AAV) [rough]

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Open design conversation. Combining rules fusing ten-discerner outputs into `apcs_unified_finding`. Opus-scale — several non-obvious decisions with downstream cascade. Two memos provide concrete input: ₢A9AAZ's fixture assay (what's happening, complementarity observations) and ₢A9AAf's field reference (authoritative per-field semantics with library-doc citations).
  
  ## Prerequisite
  
  Both container memos complete:
  
  - `Memos/memo-20260420-apck-container-fixture-assay/` — fixture assay with observed complementarity and failure modes (from ₢A9AAZ)
  - `Memos/memo-20260420-apck-container-discerner-fields.md` — authoritative per-field semantics with library citations (from ₢A9AAf)
  
  ## Reference data
  
  - `epic_initial.json` (382 KB), `epic_geriatric.json` (361 KB) — unedited container outputs on the two Epic fixtures
  - Fixture assay memo — observed complementarity, failure modes, engineering notes
  - Field-reference memo — per-field semantics, value spaces, taxonomies, known redundancies
  
  ## Questions to answer
  
  1. HOSPITAL vs ADDRESS vs GPE — triangulate Stanford HOSPITAL + Stanza FAC/GPE + regex address shape into distinct `apcs_phi_category` values.
  2. Structured-ID precedence — rule for "regex overrides model fragmentation at the same span" (SSN, encounter IDs, email, URL).
  3. spaCy entity span utility — label is always `ENTITY` (zero-info classifier), but spans carry exclusive coverage (~229/283 have no Stanza-entity overlap per fixture assay). Decide whether span-position serves as corroboration/suppression signal or is net noise against Epic-field-label false positives.
  4. Dictionary + model corroboration — how surname/firstname/city compose with Stanford PATIENT and Stanza PERSON.
  5. Confidence threshold — Stanford ≥0.95 is the high-signal band per memo; pick a cutoff and name it.
  6. Granularity — phrase-scope vs word-scope when discerners disagree on span width.
  7. Clinician toggle composition — discerner / post-reconciliation mutator / disposition override.
  8. VENDOR label — Safe Harbor question for APCAS (flagged, not resolved here).
  9. Field-level enquoining discipline — APCS0 quoin additions (wire payload records, finding-field sub-quoins) are driven by which fields combining rules actually reference. Fields unused by any rule remain prose-only in APCS0. Tiered drop candidates per earlier design conversation: Stanza token `ner` (redundant with entity variant), opaque native-format fields (`morph`/`feats`), potentially one parser's tokens if second-parser corroboration isn't cashed in.
  
  ## Deliverables
  
  - `apcs_combining` section in APCS0 authoritative (DEFERRED flags gone).
  - Pairwise rules naming specific discerner outputs — "intimate, not generic".
  - Wire payload record quoins in APCS0 (likely `apcswr_*` voiced `axj_root_structure` / `axj_structure` using section form; scope driven by rule references). Revise `apcsw_` mapping-section legend note about `r` being prose-only.
  - Cascade to APCPS if rules imply wire-format changes (likely to retire APCPS's illustrative JSON in favor of APCS0 authoritative).
  
  ## Exit criteria
  
  - APCS0 `apcs_combining` authoritative.
  - A reader can trace `apcsgt_normalized_text` → ten discerners → combining → `apcs_unified_finding` → anonymized output without gaps.
  - Every field referenced by a combining rule has a corresponding quoin in APCS0.
  - Wire payload schemas specified in APCS0 using `axj_*` section-form inline voicing (no new hierarchy-marker family needed).
  - Rust implementation deferred to ₢A9AAe (wire-protocol-impl, now scheduled to follow this pace).
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Rust code changes.
  - Discerner internals.
  - Wire-protocol hardening beyond envelope/payload schema (separate concerns).

12	
13	Ann's PHI Clipbuddy (`apcap`) — a Tauri desktop app (macOS, later Windows) that:
14	
15	1. Validates clipboard content as clinical via heuristic, clears system clipboard after consumption

Mounted ₣A9 · apck-1-proof-of-life (23/27, racing).

**Paddock shape:** Product design → prototype implementation for Ann's PHI Clipbuddy. Recent trajectory: ten-discerner restructure (₢A9AAU) → container scaffolding with two Epic fixtures (₢A9AAZ) → APCS0 quoin migration (₢A9AAc) → wire protocol spec (₢A9AAd) → container field reference memo (₢A9AAf). Two memos now in hand: fixture assay (complementarity observations) and field reference (per-field semantics with library citations).

**Next pace:** ₢A9AAV · apcs-combining-algorithm [rough] — *Open design conversation*, opus-scale.

Author the authoritative `apcs_combining` section in APCS0, fusing the ten discerners' outputs into `apcs_unified_finding`. Nine open questions span HOSPITAL/ADDRESS/GPE triangulation, regex-vs-model precedence, spaCy ENTITY utility, dictionary corroboration, confidence thresholds, granularity, clinician toggles, VENDOR category, and field-level enquoining. Deliverables include pairwise rules naming specific discerner outputs, wire payload record quoins, and APCPS cascade. Rust implementation deferred to ₢A9AAe.

**Silks assessment:** `apcs-combining-algorithm` fits the docket — algorithm in the generic design-rules sense, not a code algorithm. Keep.

**Proposed approach:**
- Ground the conversation in the two prerequisite memos before touching APCS0
- Walk the nine questions in dependency order (confidence threshold & structured-ID precedence first — they constrain downstream rules; HOSPITAL triangulation next; spaCy ENTITY utility is the swing question for field-level enquoining)
- Capture decisions inline; draft APCS0 section only after all nine land
- Cascade wire-payload quoins into APCS0, then sweep APCPS

**Execution strategy:** Sequential opus — this is a design conversation, not parallelizable work. Heavy reading of two memos and APCS0 upfront; then iterative Q&A with you.

Ready to proceed? First step would be reading both memos so I can ground the conversation in concrete observations rather than abstract principles.

1	<!--
2	Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# APCK Container — First Fixture Assay
8	
9	**Date:** 2026-04-20
10	**Pace:** ₢A9AAZ (container-scaffolding)
11	**Inputs:** `test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt` (5,147 chars), `test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt` (4,988 chars)
12	**Outputs:** `epic_initial.json` (382 KB), `epic_geriatric.json` (361 KB) — sibling files in this directory
13	**Image:** `apck-container:local` (1.73 GB, Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1-cpu + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2)
14	
15	## Why this memo exists
16	
17	The pace docket's smoke test used a single curated one-liner
18	(`"Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen."`)
19	which exercised the wire contract but not the discerners' behavior on realistic
20	clinical prose. This memo captures the first end-to-end run of both HTML
21	fixtures (after whitespace-normalization via a stdlib HTML-to-text helper that
22	mirrors `zapcrp_normalize_whitespace` in `apcrp_parse.rs`) so that subsequent
23	paces — combining, Rust-side wiring, schema pruning — can reason from evidence
24	rather than assumption.
25	
26	The two JSON files beside this memo are unedited container outputs and should
27	be treated as reference data. They are large because all three discerners emit
28	token-level findings; that size is itself a design data point (see
29	Engineering Notes below).
30	
31	## Headline result
32	
33	The container works end-to-end on realistic clinical text. The APCS0/APCPS
34	complementarity claim — Stanford strong on narrative PHI, weak on structured
35	identifiers; Stanza providing independent corroboration via OntoNotes NER;
36	spaCy contributing syntactic signal — is validated by concrete examples in
37	both outputs.
38	
39	## Stanford de-identifier — strong narrative, weak on structure
40	
41	### What it nailed (confidence ≥ 0.99)
42	
43	- **All patient + family names**: Margaret J. Thornton, Robert Thornton, Ms.
44	  Thornton, Harold W. Eriksen, Karen Eriksen-Moody, Thomas Eriksen, Miriam
45	  Eriksen, Mr. Eriksen.
46	- **All clinicians as HCW**: Dr. Susan Chen, James R. Whitfield MD, Priya
47	  Anand MD, Elena Vasquez PA-C, Naomi Okwu MD, Patricia Nowak, Dr. Anand,
48	  Dr. Whitfield.
49	- **Every date at confidence 1.0**: DOBs, visit dates, historical years —
50	  9 dates on the geriatric fixture including freestanding years (`2024`,
51	  `1961`) and month-year stubs (`04/2026`, `01/2026`).
52	- **All structured IDs that didn't fragment**: MRN `00847293`, account
53	  `3391057842`, SSN `471-83-2956`, BCBS plan `BCBS-MEM-992817445`, device
54	  serial `1234567890`, DEA-style `AW1234567`.
55	- **All phone numbers** across both fixtures.
56	
57	### Where it got confused
58	
59	- **Address ↔ facility conflation.** "1847 Cranberry Lane, Westbrook, ME
60	  04092" (home address) and "Maine Medical Center" (a real hospital) both
61	  tagged `HOSPITAL`. The model has no separate ADDRESS label — APCS0
62	  documents this and the fixture assay makes it concrete.
63	- **ID fragmentation.** `ENC-2026-0051203` on the geriatric fixture split
64	  into two findings: `ENC-` at confidence 0.98 and `-0051203` at 1.0, as
65	  separate `ID` entries. Exactly the "structured identifiers fragment
66	  across labels" failure mode described in APCS0. Combining should prefer
67	  regex evidence for structured IDs.
68	- **URL/email surgery.** Email `m.thornton47@gmail.com` fragmented into
69	  `m.thornton47` tagged PATIENT + `gmail`/`com` tagged VENDOR. Portal URL
70	  `https://mychart.mainehealth.org/patient/thornton-m` fragmented into
71	  `mychart`/`mainehealth` (VENDOR) + `thornton-m` (PATIENT). Regex for
72	  email and URL shapes handles these cleanly.
73	- **Low-confidence noise.** "SS" (0.62) tagged HOSPITAL — a stray fragment
74	  from "SSN" context. Combining can reasonably filter sub-0.7 non-name
75	  labels; every high-signal finding was ≥ 0.95.
76	
77	## spaCy — syntactic gold, NER underwhelming
78	
79	- **1,008 / 942 token findings** (POS, morph, lemma, dep, head). This is
80	  the real value of spaCy in this pipeline: feedstock for homograph
81	  disambiguation in combining (e.g., "May" as proper noun vs. modal verb).
82	- **283 / 290 entity findings, all labeled generically `ENTITY`.**
83	  `en_core_sci_md`'s default NER emits a single entity type — it was
84	  designed for biomedical entity detection, not typed classification. The
85	  NER output is therefore low-value for PHI discrimination; combining
86	  should weight spaCy by its syntactic outputs, not its entity list.
87	
88	## Stanza — complementary NER taxonomy, some leakage
89	
90	- **PERSON captures mirror Stanford exactly**: independent corroboration
91	  on every person name in both fixtures. This is the two-parser
92	  redundancy the spec calls for.
93	- **GPE** (geo-political): Westbrook, Portland, Scarborough, Cape
94	  Elizabeth, South Portland — the locality signal Stanford conflates into
95	  HOSPITAL. Combining can separate "this is a city" (GPE) from "this is
96	  a hospital" (HOSPITAL) using Stanza evidence.
97	- **FAC** (facility): addresses + Maine Medical Center, separating

99	  bucket.

101	  on medical abbreviations (NSTEMI, PCI, ED, EMS) — Stanza treats
102	  all-caps strings as organizations.
103	- **CARDINAL leakage**: `471-83-2956` (SSN) and `555-0143` (phone
104	  fragment) tagged `CARDINAL` — PHI hidden as "just a number".
105	  `207-555-0488` tagged `TIME`. Combining should recognize that
106	  structured-ID regex evidence dominates Stanza's numeric
107	  misclassifications.
108	
109	## Predictions from APCS0 / APCPS — validated
110	
111	1. **Stanford narrative strength + structured weakness** — confirmed with
112	   concrete examples.
113	2. **Two independent parsers corroborating PERSON** — confirmed.
114	   Stanford PATIENT/HCW and Stanza PERSON agree across every name in
115	   both documents.
116	3. **NER taxonomy complementarity** — confirmed. Stanza GPE, FAC,
117	   CARDINAL give combining signals Stanford lacks.
118	4. **Regex will earn its keep** — confirmed. SSN, phone, encounter ID,
119	   email, URL all parse cleaner in regex than any of the three models.
120	
121	## Engineering notes worth carrying forward
122	
123	- **JSON size: ~370 KB per normalized-page.** For the Rust consumer,
124	  this is substantial deserialization per clipboard event. Options to
125	  consider in the next pace: (a) schema-side prune in the container
126	  (e.g., drop token-level findings whose `pos` is punctuation,
127	  drop Stanza CARDINAL when the surface matches a known regex
128	  structured-ID shape); (b) Rust-side streaming parse that skips
129	  uninteresting records.
130	- **Steady-state throughput is fast.** Both fixtures processed in
131	  under a second each after warmup. The docket's 30–90 s estimate
132	  referred to cold model load; once `ready`, discerner latency per
133	  document is negligible at this text size.
134	- **Drop-old staging matters for testing.** The container processes
135	  only the highest-indexed `{N}-in.txt`. If you copy both fixtures
136	  into `~/apcjd/` before the scanner tick, only the higher-numbered
137	  file gets processed. The assay sequenced 10001 → wait for 10001.json
138	  → 10002 → wait for 10002.json. Worth calling out in the container
139	  README.
140	- **Container security posture held.** Runtime was `--network=none
141	  --cap-drop=all --read-only --user nobody:nogroup` throughout both
142	  assays. No network-exfiltration surface; PHI stayed within the
143	  bind-mount.
144	
145	## Items for subsequent paces to study
146	
147	These are flagged by the data, not prescribed. The design conversation
148	can continue from here.
149	
150	- **Combining rule for HOSPITAL vs. ADDRESS vs. GPE**: Stanford's
151	  HOSPITAL label is doing triple duty. A combining rule that triangulates
152	  Stanford HOSPITAL + Stanza FAC/GPE + regex address shape into distinct

# APCK Container — First Fixture Assay

**Date:** 2026-04-20
**Pace:** ₢A9AAZ (container-scaffolding)
**Inputs:** `test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt` (5,147 chars), `test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt` (4,988 chars)
**Outputs:** `epic_initial.json` (382 KB), `epic_geriatric.json` (361 KB) — sibling files in this directory
**Image:** `apck-container:local` (1.73 GB, Python 3.11-slim + torch 2.5.1-cpu + transformers 4.46.3 + scispaCy 0.5.5 + Stanza 1.9.2)

## Why this memo exists

The pace docket's smoke test used a single curated one-liner
(`"Patient: Margaret Thornton. DOB: 03/15/1952. Seen at Mercy General by Dr. Chen."`)
which exercised the wire contract but not the discerners' behavior on realistic
clinical prose. This memo captures the first end-to-end run of both HTML
fixtures (after whitespace-normalization via a stdlib HTML-to-text helper that
mirrors `zapcrp_normalize_whitespace` in `apcrp_parse.rs`) so that subsequent
paces — combining, Rust-side wiring, schema pruning — can reason from evidence
rather than assumption.

The two JSON files beside this memo are unedited container outputs and should
be treated as reference data. They are large because all three discerners emit
token-level findings; that size is itself a design data point (see
Engineering Notes below).

## Headline result

The container works end-to-end on realistic clinical text. The APCS0/APCPS
complementarity claim — Stanford strong on narrative PHI, weak on structured
identifiers; Stanza providing independent corroboration via OntoNotes NER;
spaCy contributing syntactic signal — is validated by concrete examples in
both outputs.

## Stanford de-identifier — strong narrative, weak on structure

### What it nailed (confidence ≥ 0.99)

- **All patient + family names**: Margaret J. Thornton, Robert Thornton, Ms.
  Thornton, Harold W. Eriksen, Karen Eriksen-Moody, Thomas Eriksen, Miriam
  Eriksen, Mr. Eriksen.
- **All clinicians as HCW**: Dr. Susan Chen, James R. Whitfield MD, Priya
  Anand MD, Elena Vasquez PA-C, Naomi Okwu MD, Patricia Nowak, Dr. Anand,
  Dr. Whitfield.
- **Every date at confidence 1.0**: DOBs, visit dates, historical years —
  9 dates on the geriatric fixture including freestanding years (`2024`,
  `1961`) and month-year stubs (`04/2026`, `01/2026`).
- **All structured IDs that didn't fragment**: MRN `00847293`, account
  `3391057842`, SSN `471-83-2956`, BCBS plan `BCBS-MEM-992817445`, device
  serial `1234567890`, DEA-style `AW1234567`.
- **All phone numbers** across both fixtures.

### Where it got confused

- **Address ↔ facility conflation.** "1847 Cranberry Lane, Westbrook, ME
  04092" (home address) and "Maine Medical Center" (a real hospital) both
  tagged `HOSPITAL`. The model has no separate ADDRESS label — APCS0
  documents this and the fixture assay makes it concrete.
- **ID fragmentation.** `ENC-2026-0051203` on the geriatric fixture split
  into two findings: `ENC-` at confidence 0.98 and `-0051203` at 1.0, as
  separate `ID` entries. Exactly the "structured identifiers fragment
  across labels" failure mode described in APCS0. Combining should prefer
  regex evidence for structured IDs.
- **URL/email surgery.** Email `m.thornton47@gmail.com` fragmented into
  `m.thornton47` tagged PATIENT + `gmail`/`com` tagged VENDOR. Portal URL
  `https://mychart.mainehealth.org/patient/thornton-m` fragmented into
  `mychart`/`mainehealth` (VENDOR) + `thornton-m` (PATIENT). Regex for
  email and URL shapes handles these cleanly.
- **Low-confidence noise.** "SS" (0.62) tagged HOSPITAL — a stray fragment
  from "SSN" context. Combining can reasonably filter sub-0.7 non-name
  labels; every high-signal finding was ≥ 0.95.

## spaCy — syntactic gold, NER underwhelming

- **1,008 / 942 token findings** (POS, morph, lemma, dep, head). This is
  the real value of spaCy in this pipeline: feedstock for homograph
  disambiguation in combining (e.g., "May" as proper noun vs. modal verb).
- **283 / 290 entity findings, all labeled generically `ENTITY`.**
  `en_core_sci_md`'s default NER emits a single entity type — it was
  designed for biomedical entity detection, not typed classification. The
  NER output is therefore low-value for PHI discrimination; combining
  should weight spaCy by its syntactic outputs, not its entity list.

## Stanza — complementary NER taxonomy, some leakage

- **PERSON captures mirror Stanford exactly**: independent corroboration
  on every person name in both fixtures. This is the two-parser
  redundancy the spec calls for.
- **GPE** (geo-political): Westbrook, Portland, Scarborough, Cape
  Elizabeth, South Portland — the locality signal Stanford conflates into
  HOSPITAL. Combining can separate "this is a city" (GPE) from "this is
  a hospital" (HOSPITAL) using Stanza evidence.
- **FAC** (facility): addresses + Maine Medical Center, separating
  facility from narrative context more cleanly than Stanford's HOSPITAL
  bucket.
- **ORG**: mostly right (Maine Medical Center) but with false positives
  on medical abbreviations (NSTEMI, PCI, ED, EMS) — Stanza treats
  all-caps strings as organizations.
- **CARDINAL leakage**: `471-83-2956` (SSN) and `555-0143` (phone
  fragment) tagged `CARDINAL` — PHI hidden as "just a number".
  `207-555-0488` tagged `TIME`. Combining should recognize that
  structured-ID regex evidence dominates Stanza's numeric
  misclassifications.

## Predictions from APCS0 / APCPS — validated

1. **Stanford narrative strength + structured weakness** — confirmed with
   concrete examples.
2. **Two independent parsers corroborating PERSON** — confirmed.
   Stanford PATIENT/HCW and Stanza PERSON agree across every name in
   both documents.
3. **NER taxonomy complementarity** — confirmed. Stanza GPE, FAC,
   CARDINAL give combining signals Stanford lacks.
4. **Regex will earn its keep** — confirmed. SSN, phone, encounter ID,
   email, URL all parse cleaner in regex than any of the three models.

## Engineering notes worth carrying forward

- **JSON size: ~370 KB per normalized-page.** For the Rust consumer,
  this is substantial deserialization per clipboard event. Options to
  consider in the next pace: (a) schema-side prune in the container
  (e.g., drop token-level findings whose `pos` is punctuation,
  drop Stanza CARDINAL when the surface matches a known regex
  structured-ID shape); (b) Rust-side streaming parse that skips
  uninteresting records.
- **Steady-state throughput is fast.** Both fixtures processed in
  under a second each after warmup. The docket's 30–90 s estimate
  referred to cold model load; once `ready`, discerner latency per
  document is negligible at this text size.
- **Drop-old staging matters for testing.** The container processes
  only the highest-indexed `{N}-in.txt`. If you copy both fixtures
  into `~/apcjd/` before the scanner tick, only the higher-numbered
  file gets processed. The assay sequenced 10001 → wait for 10001.json
  → 10002 → wait for 10002.json. Worth calling out in the container
  README.
- **Container security posture held.** Runtime was `--network=none
  --cap-drop=all --read-only --user nobody:nogroup` throughout both
  assays. No network-exfiltration surface; PHI stayed within the
  bind-mount.

## Items for subsequent paces to study

These are flagged by the data, not prescribed. The design conversation
can continue from here.

- **Combining rule for HOSPITAL vs. ADDRESS vs. GPE**: Stanford's
  HOSPITAL label is doing triple duty. A combining rule that triangulates
  Stanford HOSPITAL + Stanza FAC/GPE + regex address shape into distinct
  apcsgc_phi_category values would eliminate the conflation.
- **Structured-ID precedence**: when regex says "SSN" and Stanford says
  two fragmented IDs at the same offsets, regex wins. When regex says
  "email" and Stanford says VENDOR+PATIENT fragments, regex wins. A
  combining primitive for "regex overrides model fragmentation at the
  same span" appears load-bearing.
- **spaCy entity weight**: given all spaCy entities are generic, its
  entity findings may be net noise. Combining could weight spaCy at
  zero for entity decisions, use it only for syntactic features.
- **JSON payload pruning**: if the Rust consumer only needs tokens for
  specific homograph disambiguation cases, token-level findings could
  be filtered in the container to reduce JSON size by an order of
  magnitude. Decision depends on whether combining wants to evaluate
  token-level features broadly or only selectively.
- **VENDOR label as PHI**: `gmail`, `com`, `mychart`, `mainehealth` are
  all technically low-entropy strings caught by the VENDOR label.
  Whether Safe Harbor requires these scrubbed is an APCAS question,
  not a detection-pipeline question — but the evidence is collected
  either way.

## Reproduction

```
# HTML-to-text normalization (stdlib only; mirrors apcrp_parse whitespace collapse)
python3 Tools/apck/.../normalize.py  # ad hoc during this run

# Container lifecycle
tt/apcw-cb.ContainerBuild.sh
tt/apcw-cs.ContainerStart.sh

# Wait for readiness
until grep -q "ready" ~/apcjd/container-log.txt; do sleep 1; done

# Stage fixtures sequentially (drop-old policy requires this)
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_initial.txt   ~/apcjd/10001-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10001.json; do sleep 1; done
cp Tools/apck/test_fixtures/epic_geriatric.txt ~/apcjd/10002-in.txt
until test -f ~/apcjd/10002.json; do sleep 1; done

tt/apcw-cx.ContainerStop.sh
```

6	
7	# APCK Container — Discerner Field Reference
8	
9	**Date:** 2026-04-20
10	**Pace:** ₢A9AAf (container-discerner-fields)
11	**Prerequisite:** `Memos/memo-20260420-apck-container-fixture-assay/` (establishes "what is happening"; this memo establishes "what does each field mean")
12	**Feeds:** ₢A9AAV (combining) — design reference, not a rule list
13	**Fixtures referenced:** `epic_initial.json` (index 10001), `epic_geriatric.json` (index 10002), sibling to the fixture-assay memo
14	
15	## Scope
16	
17	Every field the container emits for each of Stanford, scispaCy, and Stanza,
18	with three layers per field:
19	
20	1. **Authoritative semantics** — what the library says the field represents.
21	2. **Observed behavior** — what actually appears in the two fixtures.
22	3. **Utility assessment** — what the field could signal in combining, plus
23	   known failure modes and redundancies.
24	
25	Out of scope: combining rules (₢A9AAV), quoin minting in APCS0 (deferred
26	until combining earns each field), Python changes, wire-vocabulary changes.
27	
28	## Version pinning
29	
30	Inherited from the fixture-assay memo. This reference is valid only for:
31	
32	| Library | Version | Model / pipeline |
33	|---|---|---|
34	| `transformers` | 4.46.3 | `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base` |
35	| `torch` | 2.5.1-cpu | (CPU inference) |
36	| `scispacy` | 0.5.5 | `en_core_sci_md` |
37	| `stanza` | 1.9.2 | English UD EWT + OntoNotes NER |
38	
39	Future upgrades date the memo; refresh in a separate pace.
40	
41	## Wire envelope (reminder)
42	
43	Per `APCPS` Container Architecture + `APCS0` Wire Protocol, each `{N}.json`
44	the container writes has shape:
45	
46	```
47	{ "index": <int>,
48	  "stanford": { "findings": [ ... ] },
49	  "spacy":    { "findings": [ ... ] },
50	  "stanza":   { "findings": [ ... ] }
51	}
52	```
53	
54	Each discerner contributes a `findings` list. The shape of each finding
55	record is per-discerner and documented below.
56	
57	All `start` / `end` offsets throughout are half-open character offsets
58	into the normalized-text input (`{N}-in.txt`), UTF-8 code-unit counts
59	over bytes that are almost entirely ASCII in Epic clipboards.
60	
61	---
62	
63	## 1. Stanford — `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base`
64	
65	### 1.1 Identity
66	
67	| Attribute | Value |
68	|---|---|
69	| Model ID | `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base` |
70	| Base architecture | PubMedBERT (BERT-uncased, biomedical pretraining) with a linear token-classification head |
71	| Task | Token-level PHI classification |
72	| Training data | 999 Stanford chest X-ray / CT reports (Nov 2019 – Nov 2020) + 3,001 previously-labeled X-ray reports + 2,193 previously-labeled clinical notes; evaluated on i2b2 2006 + i2b2 2014 |
73	| Head shape | 8 logits per subword token → softmax → argmax |
74	| Tokenizer | WordPiece (PubMedBERT-uncased); max sequence 512, greedy chunking between sentences |
75	| Paper | Chambon, Wu, Steinkamp, Adleberg, Cook, Langlotz. *Automated deidentification of radiology reports combining transformer and "hide in plain sight" rule-based methods.* JAMIA 30(2), 2022. [PMC9846681](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9846681/) |
76	| Model card | [huggingface.co/StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base](https://huggingface.co/StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
77	| Config source | [config.json id2label](https://huggingface.co/StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base/blob/main/config.json) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
78	
79	### 1.2 Container-side span fusion
80	
81	The Stanford model emits one prediction per WordPiece subtoken. Our
82	wrapper (`Tools/apck/apcd/container/discerners/stanford.py`) post-processes:
83	
84	1. Drop `(0, 0)` offset tokens (CLS / SEP / PAD).
85	2. Drop O-labeled tokens.
86	3. Fuse consecutive non-O tokens that share the same label (and are
87	   adjacent in source text) into a single span.
88	4. Span `confidence` = mean of the fused tokens' confidences.
89	
90	This post-processing is why the finding list is flat (no BIO prefixes)
91	and why each record already represents a coalesced span rather than a
92	subword.
93	
94	### 1.3 Per-field catalog
95	
96	All finding records share the same shape — no `kind` discriminator.
97	
98	| Field | Type | Semantics | Observed in fixtures |
99	|---|---|---|---|
100	| `text` | string | The character span `normalized_text[start:end]`, reproduced verbatim. | 2–44 chars per label; no newlines; 34 records contain internal spaces. |
101	| `start` | int | Half-open start offset into the normalized-text input. | Monotone non-decreasing within a finding list (container emits left-to-right by chunk). |
102	| `end` | int | Exclusive end offset. `text` length always equals `end − start`. | Confirmed: zero offset / length mismatches across 70 findings. |
103	| `label` | string | One of the 8 native labels (see §1.4). O is never emitted (our wrapper filters it). | 7 of 7 non-O labels observed. |
104	| `confidence` | float | Mean softmax probability across the fused subtokens for the predicted label. | Range `[0.6225, 1.0000]`. Concentrated above 0.95 (66/70). |
105	
106	### 1.4 Value-space: the 8-label taxonomy
107	
108	The model's head is 8-wide. Taken verbatim from the HuggingFace
109	`config.json` `id2label` mapping:
110	
111	| id | label | Paper category | HIPAA Safe Harbor relation |
112	|---|---|---|---|
113	| 0 | `O` | (not-PHI) | n/a |
114	| 1 | `VENDOR` | Vendor and software names | **Above Safe Harbor** — institutional-preference addition |
115	| 2 | `DATE` | Dates | Yes (Safe Harbor §(C)) — all date elements smaller than year |
116	| 3 | `HCW` | Provider names | Yes (names of individuals) |
117	| 4 | `HOSPITAL` | Locations | Yes (geographic subdivisions smaller than state) — also catches institution names and addresses generically |
118	| 5 | `ID` | IDs | Yes (medical record numbers, account numbers, SSNs, device identifiers, etc.) |
119	| 6 | `PATIENT` | Patient names | Yes (names of individuals) |
120	| 7 | `PHONE` | Phone numbers | Yes |
121	
122	The paper explicitly calls out that PROVIDER NAMES (our `HCW`) and
123	VENDOR AND SOFTWARE NAMES (our `VENDOR`) "exceed HIPAA Safe Harbor
124	requirements but reflect institutional preferences." AGE is **not** a
125	label in this model; it must be inferred from `DATE` evidence or from
126	Rust-side regex.
127	
128	The taxonomy is **flat** — no B-/I-/E-/S-/O-prefix scheme. The model
129	places a single label per subtoken; our wrapper fuses adjacent identical
130	labels into spans.
131	
132	### 1.5 Per-label observed behavior (fixture evidence)
133	
134	| Label | Count | Conf. min/max/mean | Width min/max/mean | Notes |
135	|---|---|---|---|---|
136	| `PATIENT` | 12 | 0.972 / 0.999 / 0.996 | 10 / 20 / 14.8 | Full names, portal-ID fragments (`m.thornton47`, `thornton-m`), honorifics. |
137	| `HCW` | 12 | 0.996 / 1.000 / 0.999 | 9 / 22 / 15.4 | "Dr." / "MD" / "PA-C" credentialed names. Tightest confidence band of any label. |
138	| `DATE` | 15 | 0.937 / 1.000 / 0.993 | 4 / 10 / 8.4 | DOBs, visit dates, freestanding years (`2026`, `1961`), month-year stubs (`04/2026`). |
139	| `ID` | 11 | 0.983 / 1.000 / 0.998 | 4 / 18 / 10.2 | MRN, SSN, BCBS plan, device serials. Fragments on hyphenated IDs (see §1.6). |
140	| `PHONE` | 5 | 0.998 / 0.999 / 0.999 | 12 / 14 / 12.8 | Parenthesized and hyphenated formats. |
141	| `HOSPITAL` | 11 | 0.623 / 1.000 / 0.954 | 2 / 44 / 26.2 | Facility names, street addresses, bare street names. Weakest confidence floor. |
142	| `VENDOR` | 4 | 0.760 / 0.923 / 0.846 | 3 / 11 / 6.5 | `gmail`, `com`, `mychart`, `mainehealth` — email / URL fragments. Weakest mean. |
143	
144	Calibration note: the paper's macro-F1 per category (Penn test set)
145	ranks categories similarly — VENDOR at 65.0 was by far the weakest,
146	HOSPITAL (labeled LOCATIONS in the paper) at 89.4 was the second-weakest,
147	all others ≥95.6. Our observed confidence band rank-orders the same way.
148	
149	### 1.6 Utility assessment — what each Stanford finding can signal
150	
151	- **`confidence ≥ 0.99`** — the high-signal band. Of 70 findings, 51 sit here.
152	  On the fixtures every confidence-≥0.99 finding is either unambiguously
153	  PHI or a fragment of PHI.
154	- **`confidence ∈ [0.95, 0.99)`** — mid-signal. 15 of 70 findings. In the
155	  fixtures these are mostly multi-word spans where one subtoken
156	  registered weaker (e.g., long addresses).
157	- **`confidence < 0.95`** — low-signal. 4 of 70. All are short-fragment
158	  VENDOR / HOSPITAL matches (`SS` at 0.623, `com` at 0.760).
159	- **`label = HOSPITAL` triple-duty.** Covers (a) real facilities like
160	  "Maine Medical Center", (b) street addresses, (c) bare street-name
161	  fragments. Any combining rule distinguishing these must triangulate
162	  with regex address shapes, Stanza `FAC`, Stanza `GPE`, or both —
163	  Stanford alone cannot.
164	- **`label = ID` fragmentation on hyphenated forms.** Example from
165	  fixture 10002: `ENC-2026-0051203` splits into `ENC-` (conf 0.98) and
166	  `-0051203` (conf 1.00), each a separate `ID` record. Any span with
167	  `text` ending or starting with `-` should be suspected a fragment and
168	  cross-checked against regex evidence.
169	- **`label = PATIENT` on email local-part / URL path.** Emails
170	  fragment: `m.thornton47@gmail.com` becomes three records — `PATIENT
171	  m.thornton47` + `VENDOR gmail` + `VENDOR com`. Portal URLs fragment
172	  similarly. Regex for email / URL shapes is the cleaner signal at the
173	  same span.
174	- **`label = VENDOR` as low-entropy strings.** The entire observed set
175	  is `{gmail, com, mychart, mainehealth}`. Whether these are PHI under
176	  Safe Harbor is an APCAS question; the signal exists either way.
177	- **Span character offsets are trustworthy.** Zero mismatches across 70
178	  findings between `end − start` and `len(text)`. Same-span overlap with
179	  Rust-side regex findings is a reliable combining primitive.

183	## 2. scispaCy — `en_core_sci_md`
184	
185	### 2.1 Identity
186	
187	| Attribute | Value |
188	|---|---|
189	| Model ID | `en_core_sci_md` |
190	| Size | ~360k vocabulary, 50k word vectors |
191	| Pipeline components | tokenizer, POS tagger, dependency parser, entity span detector ("mention detector"), lemmatizer |
192	| POS / parser training | GENIA 1.0 Treebank (converted to Universal Dependencies) + OntoNotes 5.0 |
193	| NER training | MedMentions (mention spans only — no fine-grained types) |
194	| Tokenizer | Custom rules layered on spaCy's English rule-based tokenizer, tuned for biomedical abbreviations and chemical/measurement strings |
195	| Paper | Neumann, King, Beltagy, Ammar. *ScispaCy: Fast and Robust Models for Biomedical Natural Language Processing.* BioNLP 2019. [aclanthology.org/W19-5034](https://aclanthology.org/W19-5034.pdf) |
196	| Model index | [allenai.github.io/scispacy](https://allenai.github.io/scispacy/) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
197	| Source | [github.com/allenai/scispacy](https://github.com/allenai/scispacy) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
198	
199	### 2.2 Why NER emits a single generic `ENTITY` label
200	
201	scispaCy distinguishes two model families:
202	
203	- **Full-pipeline "sci" models** (`en_core_sci_sm/md/lg/scibert`) — include
204	  a *mention detector* trained on MedMentions, which labels spans only
205	  with a single generic type because MedMentions was built for entity
206	  linking (downstream UMLS match) rather than typed classification.
207	- **Specialized NER models** (`en_ner_craft_md`, `en_ner_jnlpba_md`,
208	  `en_ner_bc5cdr_md`, `en_ner_bionlp13cg_md`) — typed, per-corpus
209	  (chemicals, diseases, proteins, etc.).
210	
211	Our container uses the full-pipeline `en_core_sci_md`, so every
212	`kind: entity` finding carries `label: "ENTITY"`. This is a design
213	consequence of MedMentions, not a defect.
214	
215	### 2.3 Per-field catalog — `kind: "token"`
216	
217	Emitted by iterating `nlp(text)` and skipping `tok.is_space` tokens
218	(see `spacy_scan.py:22`). Each record has 10 keys:
219	
220	| Field | Type | Semantics | Observed in fixtures |
221	|---|---|---|---|
222	| `kind` | string constant | Literal `"token"`. | 1,950 token records across the two fixtures. |
223	| `text` | string | `tok.text` — the surface form. | Includes punctuation, abbreviations, medical units. |
224	| `start` | int | `tok.idx` — character offset of token's first char in the full document. | Monotone non-decreasing. |
225	| `end` | int | `tok.idx + len(tok.text)`. `end − start` always equals `len(text)` (0 mismatches observed). | |

228	| `morph` | string | `str(tok.morph)` — pipe-separated `Key=Value` pairs from the Universal Features inventory, empty string when no features apply. | 29 distinct values, 318 empties. |
229	| `lemma` | string | `tok.lemma_` — dictionary base form. | Lowercase for most content words; punctuation lemma matches surface. |

247	
248	#### `pos` — Universal POS (UPOS) v2
249	
250	All 17 UPOS tags ([universaldependencies.org/u/pos](https://universaldependencies.org/u/pos/), accessed 2026-04-20):
251	
252	- **Open class** — ADJ, ADV, INTJ, NOUN, PROPN, VERB
253	- **Closed class** — ADP, AUX, CCONJ, DET, NUM, PART, PRON, SCONJ
254	- **Other** — PUNCT, SYM, X
255	
256	Observed in fixtures: 16 of 17 (INTJ absent — consistent with clinical
257	prose).
258	
259	#### `tag` — Penn-Treebank-derived (extended)
260	
261	33 distinct observed: `$ , -LRB- -RRB- . : CC CD DT HYPH IN JJ MD NN
262	NNP NNPS NNS POS PRP PRP$ RB RP SYM TO VB VBD VBG VBN VBP VBZ WP WRB
263	XX`. Extensions beyond strict PTB include `HYPH` (hyphen), `XX`
264	(unknown), and `$ SYM` as disjoint categories. Standard PTB reference
265	aside, spaCy's effective English inventory is documented in its tag
266	schema JSON shipped with the model.
267	
268	#### `dep` — Universal Dependencies v2
269	
270	All UD relations ([universaldependencies.org/u/dep](https://universaldependencies.org/u/dep/), accessed 2026-04-20), 33 observed:
271	
272	Core arguments — `nsubj obj iobj csubj ccomp xcomp`
273	Non-core dependents — `obl vocative expl dislocated advcl advmod discourse aux cop mark`
274	Nominal dependents — `nmod appos nummod acl amod det clf case`

276	MWE — `fixed flat compound`
277	Loose — `list parataxis orphan goeswith reparandum`

281	`compound:prt`, `nmod:npmod`, `nmod:poss`, `nmod:tmod`. Also the

283	spaCy's GENIA-trained parser — compare Stanza's UD-v2 `aux:pass`,
284	`nsubj:pass`). This difference matters when cross-referencing spaCy
285	and Stanza dep labels.
286	
287	Note: spaCy emits `ROOT` (all caps); Stanza emits `root` (lowercase).
288	Both mean the same thing but are not byte-equal.

291	
292	Pipe-separated `Key=Value` pairs, empty string when no features apply.
293	All 29 distinct values observed (top 15 by count):
294	
295	| Value | Count |
296	|---|---|
297	| `Number=Sing` | 761 |
298	| `` (empty) | 318 |
299	| `NumType=Card` | 255 |

313	The inventory is open-ended — it can grow as the pipeline encounters
314	more complex English. Any Rust-side consumer should parse the string
315	at `|` then `=` boundaries rather than assume a fixed lookup set.
316	
317	#### `head` — document-absolute token index
318	
319	spaCy's `token.head` returns a token object; `tok.head.i` is its
320	absolute position in the spaCy `Doc`. A root token self-references
321	(`head == self_index`). On fixture 10001: 5 tokens self-reference (the
322	5 ROOT sentences). Head indices can exceed our filtered token count
323	because the `Doc` iterator includes space tokens that we drop — so an
324	apparent "head points past the end of findings" is expected behavior,
325	not corruption.
326	
327	**Cross-library caveat.** Stanza's `head` uses CoNLL-U convention
328	(sentence-relative, 1-based, 0 = root). These two conventions are
329	incompatible byte-for-byte. Any combining primitive that walks
330	dependency trees needs per-library handling. See §4.1.

332	### 2.6 Observed behavior (fixture evidence)
333	
334	- **Token count.** 1,008 (fixture 10001) + 942 (fixture 10002). Token
335	  findings account for ~77% of all spaCy finding records.
336	- **Entity count.** 283 (10001) + 290 (10002). All 573 carry the
337	  `ENTITY` label.
338	- **Empty `morph`.** 318 of 1,950 tokens (16.3%). These are mostly
339	  `PUNCT` / `SYM` / `X` where inflection does not apply.
340	- **`pos=PROPN` density.** 251 tokens = 12.9% — high, reflecting the
341	  clinical-prose domain (patient names, facility names, provider
342	  names, city names, drug names).
343	- **Token-level start/end integrity.** Zero mismatches between
344	  `end − start` and `len(text)`.
345	- **Root count vs sentence count.** 5 sentences in fixture 10001 per
346	  `head==self_index` self-references among the filtered findings.
347	  (Stanza reports 125 sentence roots on the same text — the two
348	  sentence segmenters disagree dramatically; see §4.2.)
349	
350	### 2.7 Utility assessment
351	
352	- **Syntactic features are the reason to keep spaCy.** The mention
353	  detector's single-label output means spaCy entities are weight-zero
354	  for PHI classification. But `pos`, `tag`, `morph`, and the dependency
355	  tree give combining homograph-disambiguation handles that neither
356	  Stanford nor Stanza expose at the token level in quite the same way.
357	- **`pos=PROPN` is the strongest single-token PHI prior.** 251 tokens
358	  in the fixtures; the overwhelming majority correspond to Stanford
359	  `PATIENT` / `HCW` / `HOSPITAL` spans. A token outside any Stanford
360	  span that nonetheless has `pos=PROPN` is a candidate "missed
361	  proper noun" to cross-check.
362	- **`tag=NNP` / `NNPS` redundant with `pos=PROPN`.** One of the two can
363	  carry the signal; keeping both costs JSON bytes but simplifies
364	  per-library lookup by the Rust consumer. Not load-bearing yet — will
365	  remain until combining has reason to prefer one.
366	- **`dep=compound` clusters are a name-boundary signal.** 388 tokens in
367	  the fixtures labeled `compound`. A contiguous run of `compound`-related
368	  tokens all with `pos=PROPN` is structural evidence for a multi-word
369	  name, even when the NER models miss the boundary.
370	- **`morph=Number=Sing` on a number-looking token is a weak signal it
371	  is being treated as a noun, not a digit.** Useful for disambiguating
372	  "2" in "age 2" (NUM, NumType=Card) from "2" in "H2" (X or NOUN
373	  depending on context).
374	- **Entities are net noise for PHI.** 573 generic `ENTITY` records
375	  carrying no type information duplicate token offsets and cost JSON
376	  bytes. The container could safely emit them under a suppression flag
377	  if JSON size becomes a concern; today they are kept for completeness.
378	- **`start` / `end` trustworthy.** No offset mismatches. Same-span
379	  cross-check with Stanford and Rust-regex evidence is a reliable
380	  combining primitive.

385	
386	### 3.1 Identity
387	
388	| Attribute | Value |
389	|---|---|
390	| Library | `stanza` 1.9.2 |
391	| Language | English (`lang="en"`) |
392	| Processors loaded | `tokenize, pos, lemma, depparse, ner` (entrypoint forces these five) |
393	| Tokenize / POS / lemma / depparse corpus | Universal Dependencies English EWT (default `package="default"` for the UD stack) |
394	| NER corpus | OntoNotes 5.0 (default English NER model when the package resolves the general pipeline) |
395	| NER encoding | **BIOES** (Begin, Inside, Outside, End, Single) |
396	| Data version | UD v2.12 feature inventory |
397	| Documentation | [stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza](https://stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza/) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
398	| NER docs | [stanza/ner_models.html](https://stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza/ner_models.html) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
399	| POS docs | [stanza/pos.html](https://stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza/pos.html) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
400	| Depparse docs | [stanza/depparse.html](https://stanfordnlp.github.io/stanza/depparse.html) (accessed 2026-04-20) |
401	
402	### 3.2 Iteration model
403	
404	Our wrapper (`stanza_scan.py`) iterates `doc.sentences`, then `sent.words`.
405	For each `Word` we consult its owning `Token` (`word.parent`) to recover
406	*character* offsets (word objects themselves carry only sentence-local
407	indices) and the token-level NER tag.
408	
409	A consequence: in English multi-word-token (MWT) cases — specifically
410	possessive enclitics — two `Word` records share the *same* `parent` and
411	therefore the same character span. This is visible in our fixtures as
412	six records where `end − start ≠ len(text)`:
413	
414	| Fixture | Token span chars | Word text | Word length |
415	|---|---|---|---|
416	| 10001 | 1837..1846 (9) | `patient` | 7 |
417	| 10001 | 1837..1846 (9) | `'s` | 2 |
418	| 10001 | 2032..2039 (7) | `Anand` | 5 |
419	| 10001 | 2032..2039 (7) | `'s` | 2 |
420	| 10002 | 855..864 (9) | `patient` | 7 |
421	| 10002 | 855..864 (9) | `'s` | 2 |
422	
423	Combining must not assume Stanza `start`/`end`/`text` form a tight
424	invariant the way Stanford and spaCy do. A pair of Stanza tokens with
425	**identical start/end** is the MWT-sibling signature.
426	
427	### 3.3 Per-field catalog — `kind: "token"`
428	
429	| Field | Type | Semantics | Observed |
430	|---|---|---|---|
431	| `kind` | string constant | Literal `"token"`. | 1,953 token records. |
432	| `text` | string | `word.text` — the surface form of the syntactic word (after MWT expansion). | See §3.2 for MWT caveat. |
433	| `start` | int | `parent.start_char` — character offset of the **owning token** (not the word). | Monotone within sentence; identical for MWT siblings. |
434	| `end` | int | `parent.end_char` — same caveat. | |
435	| `upos` | string | Universal POS tag. 17 possible values. | 16 distinct (INTJ absent). |
436	| `xpos` | string | Language-specific POS — for English, Penn-Treebank-derived tags from EWT. | 34 distinct. |
437	| `feats` | string | Universal Features, pipe-separated `Key=Value`, empty string if none. | 31 distinct. |
438	| `lemma` | string | Dictionary base form. | Lowercase for most content words. |
439	| `head` | int | **Sentence-relative 1-based index**; `0` marks the root. Per CoNLL-U convention. | Range `[0, 49]` on fixture 10001 (max sentence length in words); 125 roots on that fixture. |
440	| `deprel` | string | UD v2 dependency relation. Subtype-colon-suffixed forms appear (`aux:pass`, `nsubj:pass`, `compound:prt`, etc.). | 38 distinct. |
441	| `ner` | string | Token-level BIOES-encoded NER tag (`O` or `{B,I,E,S}-LABEL`). Inherited from `parent.ner`; identical for MWT siblings. | 36 distinct composite values. |

456	
457	#### `upos` — same UPOS v2 inventory as scispaCy
458	
459	17 tags, 16 observed (INTJ absent). See §2.5 for the list.
460	
461	#### `xpos` — Penn-Treebank-derived (EWT-trained)
462	
463	34 distinct observed: `, -LRB- -RRB- . : ADD CC CD DT HYPH IN JJ LS
464	MD NFP NN NNP NNPS NNS POS PRP PRP$ RB RP SYM TO VB VBD VBG VBN VBP
465	VBZ WP WRB`. EWT-specific extensions include `ADD` (web address /
466	email), `LS` (list item marker), `NFP` (superfluous punctuation),

469	**spaCy vs Stanza tag inventories differ**:
470	
471	| Only spaCy `tag` | Only Stanza `xpos` | Both |
472	|---|---|---|
473	| `$`, `XX` | `ADD`, `LS`, `NFP` | the other 31 |

475	Do not assume spaCy `tag` and Stanza `xpos` are byte-equal even when
476	they agree in meaning.
477	
478	#### `feats` — Universal Features (EWT-trained)
479	
480	31 distinct observed (top 15 by count):
481	
482	| Value | Count |
483	|---|---|
484	| `Number=Sing` | 756 |
485	| `` (empty) | 675 |
486	| `NumForm=Digit\|NumType=Card` | 172 |
487	| `Degree=Pos` | 100 |
488	| `Number=Plur` | 75 |
489	| `Tense=Past\|VerbForm=Part` | 32 |
490	| `Mood=Ind\|Number=Sing\|Person=3\|Tense=Pres\|VerbForm=Fin` | 20 |
491	| `Mood=Imp\|VerbForm=Fin` | 20 |
492	| `NumForm=Digit\|NumType=Frac` | 17 |
493	| `VerbForm=Ger` | 15 |
494	| `Definite=Def\|PronType=Art` | 13 |
495	| `Mood=Ind\|Number=Sing\|Person=3\|Tense=Past\|VerbForm=Fin` | 13 |
496	| `Case=Gen\|Gender=Fem\|Number=Sing\|Person=3\|Poss=Yes\|PronType=Prs` | 6 |
497	| `Definite=Ind\|PronType=Art` | 5 |
498	| `NumForm=Word\|NumType=Card` | 5 |

500	**`NumForm=Digit` is load-bearing.** Unlike spaCy, Stanza EWT splits
501	cardinal numerals into `NumForm=Digit` vs `NumForm=Word`. Every
502	structured identifier candidate (SSN fragments, phone fragments, MRN
503	digits, dates-as-digits) carries `NumForm=Digit|NumType=Card`. This is
504	a token-level "is this a digit sequence" signal that is cheaper than
505	regex.

507	Note the empty-count (675) is much higher than spaCy's (318). Part of
508	the difference is MWT siblings (6 records) and part is EWT's slightly
509	more conservative feature annotation for non-inflected word classes.
510	
511	#### `deprel` — UD v2 (EWT inventory)
512	
513	38 distinct observed, including: `acl acl:relcl advcl advmod amod
514	appos aux aux:pass case cc ccomp compound compound:prt conj cop dep
515	det fixed flat list mark nmod nmod:npmod nmod:poss nmod:tmod nsubj
516	nsubj:pass nummod obj obl obl:agent obl:npmod obl:tmod parataxis
517	punct root vocative xcomp`.

519	Differences from spaCy (our observed sets):
520	
521	| spaCy only | Stanza only | Meaning |
522	|---|---|---|
523	| `auxpass`, `nsubjpass`, `dobj`, `prep`, `intj`, `neg` | `aux:pass`, `nsubj:pass`, `obj`, `flat`, `list`, `obl`, `obl:agent`, `obl:npmod`, `obl:tmod`, `fixed`, `vocative` | spaCy's English parser emits UD-v1-style relation names; Stanza EWT emits UD-v2-style relation names. |
524	
525	For combining, any rule that keys on `dep` / `deprel` must either
526	normalize across the two libraries or key on UPOS/XPOS instead. A
527	cheap normalization: `replace("auxpass","aux:pass"); replace("nsubjpass","nsubj:pass"); replace("dobj","obj"); replace("prep","case")`.
528	
529	#### `ner` — BIOES-encoded OntoNotes
530	
531	Token-level NER tag has shape `O` | `B-LABEL` | `I-LABEL` | `E-LABEL`
532	| `S-LABEL`. **BIOES** rather than plain BIO:
533	
534	- `B-` begin a multi-token entity
535	- `I-` interior of a multi-token entity
536	- `E-` end of a multi-token entity
537	- `S-` single-token entity (no B/E pair needed)
538	- `O` not in any entity
539	
540	Observed prefix counts on fixture set: `O` 1,534, `B` 100, `I` 64,
541	`E` 100, `S` 155. `B`-count equals `E`-count (as invariant demands) —
542	every multi-token entity has both endpoints. `S`-count (155) is
543	larger than `B`-count (100), so most entities in this clinical text
544	are single-token.
545	
546	Distinct composite values observed (36 total): `O` plus B/I/E/S
547	variants of `CARDINAL, DATE, FAC, GPE, ORG, PERCENT, PERSON, PRODUCT,
548	QUANTITY, TIME`. Zero hits for `I-GPE`, `B-TIME`, `I-CARDINAL` etc. —
549	expected for short spans.
550	
551	#### Entity `label` — OntoNotes 5.0, 18 possible
552	
553	Full OntoNotes inventory: `PERSON NORP FAC ORG GPE LOC PRODUCT EVENT
554	WORK_OF_ART LAW LANGUAGE DATE TIME PERCENT MONEY QUANTITY ORDINAL
555	CARDINAL`.
556	
557	Observed in our fixtures: `PERSON CARDINAL FAC GPE ORG DATE QUANTITY
558	TIME PRODUCT PERCENT` (10 of 18). Absent from fixtures but possible
559	in other Epic text: `NORP` (ethnicities/nationalities), `LOC`
560	(non-GPE locations — bodies of water, landforms), `EVENT`,
561	`WORK_OF_ART`, `LAW`, `LANGUAGE`, `MONEY`, `ORDINAL`. Any combining
562	rule that whitelists or blacklists labels should enumerate the full
563	18, not only the observed 10.
564	
565	#### `head` — CoNLL-U sentence-relative
566	
567	**Not compatible with spaCy's `head`**. Range in fixtures was `[0,
568	49]` (max sentence length in words). Value `0` = "this word is its
569	sentence's root." To dereference into an absolute position: walk
570	`doc.sentences`, track each sentence's first-word absolute index, add
571	`head − 1`. In `stanza_scan.py` we flatten to `findings` without
572	preserving sentence boundaries, so reconstructing absolute head
573	indices requires a Rust-side pass that segments by `deprel == "root"`
574	(sentence roots) and relinks. If combining does not care about
575	dependency-tree walking, the raw value is still useful as a local
576	ordinal.
577	
578	### 3.6 Observed behavior (fixture evidence)
579	
580	- **Sentence count discrepancy vs spaCy.** 125 `head=0` records on
581	  fixture 10001 (Stanza sentence count) vs 5 `head==self_index`
582	  records from spaCy on the same text. Stanza's EWT tokenizer /
583	  sentence segmenter is aggressive — it treats many line-break and
584	  bullet-formatted clinical fragments as separate sentences where
585	  spaCy's scientific tokenizer treats the document more holistically.
586	- **Entity / token ratio.** 254 entities across 1,953 tokens (13%) —
587	  significantly lower than spaCy's 29.4%, because Stanza's OntoNotes
588	  model is pickier about what constitutes an entity, while spaCy's
589	  MedMentions mention detector is permissive.
590	- **PERSON corroboration.** 41 `PERSON` entities on the two fixtures
591	  cover essentially the same spans as Stanford's `PATIENT` + `HCW`.
592	  This is the intended redundancy.
593	- **FAC vs GPE vs ORG triangulation.** `FAC` (10) = addresses + "Maine
594	  Medical Center"; `GPE` (12) = cities + state abbreviations; `ORG`
595	  (29) = Maine Medical Center + medical abbreviations treated as
596	  organizations (NSTEMI, EMS, ED, CBC). Stanford conflates all three
597	  into `HOSPITAL` — Stanza gives combining three distinct signals.
598	- **CARDINAL leakage onto PHI.** 100 `S-CARDINAL` tokens include SSN
599	  fragments (`471-83-2956` as CARDINAL) and phone-number fragments
600	  (`207` / `555-0143`). Structured-ID regex remains the clean signal
601	  at those spans.
602	- **TIME leakage.** `207-555-0488` (a phone) and `18.2 seconds` (a
603	  measurement) both tagged `TIME`. Stanza's TIME label is under-
604	  disciplined on hyphenated numbers.
605	- **Offset integrity elsewhere.** Only 6 records show
606	  `end − start ≠ len(text)`, all accounted for by MWT expansion. No
607	  other offset corruption.
608	
609	### 3.7 Utility assessment
610	
611	- **`entity.label = PERSON`** — high-signal PHI corroborator; near-
612	  1:1 overlap with Stanford PATIENT / HCW. A PERSON span outside any
613	  Stanford span flags a potential miss.
614	- **`entity.label = GPE` / `FAC`** — the disambiguation Stanford lacks.
615	  Combining can distinguish "home address" from "hospital name" from
616	  "city mention" using GPE + FAC + regex address shape together.
617	- **`entity.label = ORG`** — **conservative weight**. Real organizations
618	  (Maine Medical Center, Blue Cross) co-occur with medical-
619	  abbreviation false positives (NSTEMI, ED, EMS, CBC). Any combining
620	  rule on ORG should cross-check against a medical-abbreviation
621	  whitelist before acting.
622	- **`entity.label = CARDINAL` / `TIME` / `PRODUCT`** — **suppressive
623	  weight**. These leak onto structured identifiers and drug names
624	  that regex + Stanford already cover better. Use them as tie-
625	  breakers, not primary signals.
626	- **Token-level `ner`** — redundant with `entity` findings except in
627	  BIOES precision: the token tag distinguishes endpoints, which
628	  entity findings do not. If combining ever needs "is this token the
629	  first subtoken of an entity?" the `B-` / `S-` prefix answers cheaply.
630	- **`feats` — `NumForm=Digit`** — load-bearing for "is this surely a
631	  digit sequence, not a word-form?". Cheaper than regex for the
632	  "contains-only-digits" check, and distinguishable from `NumForm=Word`
633	  ("two") which a regex `\d+` would miss.
634	- **`upos=PROPN` density** — comparable to spaCy's signal (257 in
635	  Stanza vs 251 in spaCy) at effectively the same tokens. Either
636	  library's `PROPN` tag is a usable proper-noun prior; picking one
637	  for combining keeps JSON size down.
638	- **`deprel`** — usable, but normalize across UD v1 / v2 naming (see
639	  §3.5 deprel table). `deprel=flat` and `deprel=compound` on
640	  `upos=PROPN` sequences are strong name-boundary signals.
641	
642	---
643	
644	## 4. Cross-library semantic differences worth flagging
645	
646	### 4.1 `head` is not the same field in spaCy and Stanza
647	
648	| Library | Indexing | Zero means | Range |
649	|---|---|---|---|
650	| spaCy | Absolute document token index (`tok.head.i`) | First token in document is its own ROOT | `[0, doc_token_count_inclusive_of_spaces]` |
651	| Stanza | Sentence-relative, 1-based (CoNLL-U convention) | Current word is the sentence root | `[0, max_sentence_length_in_words]` |
652	
653	Any Rust-side tree walk must branch on discerner. The empirical tell
654	is that Stanza's `head` is bounded by sentence length (~50) while
655	spaCy's is bounded by document length (~1000+).
656	
657	### 4.2 Sentence segmentation disagrees
658	
659	spaCy's scientific tokenizer and Stanza's EWT tokenizer produce
660	dramatically different sentence counts on the same normalized Epic
661	text (5 vs 125 on fixture 10001). Neither is "wrong"; each reflects
662	its training corpus's conventions. Combining rules keyed on "is this
663	token in the same sentence as X" will give different answers per
664	library.
665	
666	### 4.3 UD dependency relation names differ in version
667	
668	spaCy emits UD v1 names (`auxpass`, `nsubjpass`, `dobj`, `prep`);
669	Stanza EWT emits UD v2 names (`aux:pass`, `nsubj:pass`, `obj`,
670	`case`). Bytes do not match even when meaning does. Normalize before
671	comparing.
672	
673	### 4.4 Penn Treebank tag inventories differ
674	
675	spaCy has `$` and `XX`; Stanza has `ADD`, `LS`, `NFP`. No combining
676	rule should assume "spaCy `tag` and Stanza `xpos` are interchangeable."
677	
678	### 4.5 ROOT capitalization
679	
680	spaCy emits `dep="ROOT"`; Stanza emits `deprel="root"`. Rust-side
681	combining should use case-insensitive comparison or normalize to one
682	form.
683	
684	### 4.6 Entity label conventions differ
685	
686	| Library | Label inventory | Observed distinct labels |
687	|---|---|---|
688	| Stanford | 8 flat native labels | 7 (no O in output) |
689	| scispaCy | Single `ENTITY` label | 1 |
690	| Stanza | OntoNotes 18 (via BIOES) | 10 |
691	
692	scispaCy entities cannot be filtered by type; Stanza entities can.
693	Stanford has its own vocabulary. Treat the three as three independent
694	label spaces — do not attempt a unified label enum across them.
695	
696	---
697	
698	## 5. Consolidated field index
699	
700	Quick-lookup table. If you are staring at a record in `epic_*.json`
701	and want to know what a field means, use this as the jump point.
702	
703	| Discerner | Shape | Field | Jump to |
704	|---|---|---|---|
705	| stanford | (flat) | `text` | §1.3 |
706	| stanford | (flat) | `start` / `end` | §1.3 |
707	| stanford | (flat) | `label` | §1.4 |
708	| stanford | (flat) | `confidence` | §1.3, §1.5 |
709	| spacy | token | `text`, `start`, `end` | §2.3 |
710	| spacy | token | `pos` | §2.3, §2.5 |
711	| spacy | token | `tag` | §2.3, §2.5 |
712	| spacy | token | `morph` | §2.3, §2.5 |
713	| spacy | token | `lemma` | §2.3 |
714	| spacy | token | `head` | §2.3, §2.5, §4.1 |
715	| spacy | token | `dep` | §2.3, §2.5 |
716	| spacy | entity | `label` | §2.2, §2.4, §4.6 |
717	| stanza | token | `text`, `start`, `end` | §3.2, §3.3 |
718	| stanza | token | `upos` | §3.3, §3.5 |
719	| stanza | token | `xpos` | §3.3, §3.5, §4.4 |
720	| stanza | token | `feats` | §3.3, §3.5 |
721	| stanza | token | `lemma` | §3.3 |
722	| stanza | token | `head` | §3.3, §3.5, §4.1 |
723	| stanza | token | `deprel` | §3.3, §3.5, §4.3 |
724	| stanza | token | `ner` | §3.3, §3.5 |
725	| stanza | entity | `label` | §3.4, §3.5, §4.6 |
726	
727	---
728	
729	## 6. Exit criteria checklist
730	
731	- [x] Every field present in `epic_initial.json` and `epic_geriatric.json`
732	  has a reference entry (see §1.3, §2.3, §2.4, §3.3, §3.4 and §5).
733	- [x] Each library's authoritative documentation is cited with URL and
734	  access date (§1.1, §2.1, §3.1, and cross-refs in §2.5, §3.5).
735	- [x] Cross-library semantic differences (which will bite combining)
736	  are called out as a dedicated section (§4).
737	- [x] Memo locks to pinned versions (§"Version pinning").
738	- [x] Out-of-scope items not touched: no combining rules written, no
739	  APCS0 quoin minting, no Python changes.
740
